Copper has become the world’s third most consumed metal, but uncertain demand levels and supply constraints have created a volatile outlook. Presented by CME Group

A recent survey by the Tucson Metro Chamber reveals that 72 percent of Arizonans support copper mining in the Copper State, highlighting widespread recognition of copper’s vital role in modern life. As a key component in renewable energy technologies, electric vehicles, and everyday electronics, copper is integral to the transition toward a more sustainable future. 

Accounting for 74 percent of the nations' copper, Arizona holds a strategic position in ensuring domestic copper production, which is crucial for reducing dependence on foreign imports. This not only bolsters economic security but also strengthens the nation’s supply chain resilience, particularly in industries that are critical to U.S. infrastructure and national security.

By tapping into Arizona’s rich copper reserves, Resolution Copper will help the U.S. achieve greater self-sufficiency in this essential metal, supporting the growth of emerging technologies and furthering national goals in clean energy, transportation, and defense innovation.

Operating for over 120 years, the Kennecott Mine is the second-largest copper producer in the United States and supplies domestically sourced copper, gold, silver, molybdenum, and tellurium both nationally and internationally. 

The mine is located in the Oquirrh Mountains near Salt Lake City, Utah, and is operated by Rio Tinto. Mining activities began in 1903, making it one of the oldest continuously operating mines in the U.S. and the world's deepest open-pit mine. To date, Kennecott has produced over 19 million tons of copper, along with substantial amounts of other valuable metals. 

At its peak, the mine employed thousands and continues to provide around 2,400 jobs, bolstering the local economy through direct employment and support of associated industries.

The 19 million tons of copper produced is enough to supplement over 4 million 3 MW wind turbines. To put it in perspective, it’s estimated that the U.S. currently has 90,000 wind turbines operating today.

Copper from Kennecott is vital to daily life, playing an essential role in electrical wiring, plumbing, and infrastructure. Its superior conductivity makes it indispensable in electronic devices like smartphones, computers, and home appliances. Beyond that, copper is crucial to the renewable energy sector, as it is used extensively in wind turbines, solar panels, and electric vehicles, making it integral to the shift towards a more sustainable energy future. 

To learn more about Kennecott, please visit Rio Tinto’s Website via the link below:
https://lnkd.in/eF4vKZSh

Meet John and Erma Evans, residents of a small rural town in Maine who have embraced clean energy solutions in their home. Over 20 years they developed a vision for building their dream home powered by the sun and innovative clean energy technologies.
 
The Evanses have embraced electric cars because they are practical, cheaper to fuel and maintain, and help reduce pollution. They live at the top of a very steep dirt road and have found that their electric car and truck can get the job done, lugging, tugging, or just getting around town. A rooftop solar system powers their electric vehicles, which are then able to provide them with backup power if it's ever needed while still being able to travel everywhere they need to get, including across the country.
 
Your EV is more than just a car. It's actually a battery on wheels with the power to support the whole energy system!
 
⚡EV batteries can store a huge amount of energy, even enough to power your home for days.
🌙 Charging overnight when demand is low helps to flatten peak electricity demand and reduce stress on the electric grid. That means that there are fewer costly upgrades and lowers rates for everyone, making our systems cleaner and more reliable.
💸Having a more balanced grid demand means more reliable energy, and lower costs for everyone.
 
So when you drive electric you're actually cutting pollution and making our grid stronger and smarter for the future. John and Erma are a great example of what is possible and that EV's are a great option for most of us! 
 
It's not just about saving on gas or cutting emissions but being a part of the shift to building a cleaner, stronger, and more affordable energy future for us all!

With Weichen Wang @wangweichen0520,
Youssef Denial @youssefindenial, our proposal for Renewable Energy Research Center of Iceland was awarded 3rd Prize among other inspiring submissions for international architecture competition (unbuilt), organized by @designunlimitedonline.

From Jules Verne's <Journey to the center of the Earth> to the whispered legends of Iceland's Huldufólk, the human curiosity to descend into the depths of the Earth and draw upon its elemental power has long shaped our cultural imagination.

Such fascination finds a contemporary expression in a vision for a renewable energy research center: an archaeology of the future, where science, myth, and landscape converge.

Sited on the northwest end of Kársnes habror peninsula, the 16,000-square-meter institution is conceived as a clustered village of mass timber modules, assembled along a Voronoi grid that echoes the beauty of basalt formations. Within, it weaves together geothermal gardens, interior civic spaces, and vibrant waterfront promenades-each a stage for knowledge, encounter, and ecological stewardship. Entirely electric and self-sufficient, the structure draws from the site's own elemental forces -sun, wind, and geothermal heat-becoming both a generator and a testament to a new symbiosis between architecture and the land it's built on.

page_name: North Country Alliance for Balanced Change
ad_creative_bodies:
Residents, Property Owner Weigh In After Landfill Application Denied
Casella Avoids Direct Questions As Project’s Fate More Uncertain

By:Robert Blechl

Amid a 6-year fight, opponents of a commercial landfill in Dalton are cautiously optimistic after the company proposing it encountered a big setback and is not revealing specifics regarding its next steps.

On Thursday, after issuing four letters to Casella Waste Systems informing it that its standard solid waste permit application is incomplete, state permitting officials have again said that the company has still not provided the information and the application is now denied.

DES’s rejection comes after Casella has spent years trying to gain support for siting its Granite State Landfill LLC beside Forest Lake State Park, a proposal that has mobilized opposition across the North Country, across the state, and both parties at the New Hampshire statehouse.

Because of the repeated incompleteness, Casella’s permit application became effectively dormant on Feb. 28, the day that Casella submitted its latest application, which the New Hampshire Department of Environmental Services, in its Thursday denial letter, said still doesn’t contain the legal papers, property agreements, and other required documents that it has been requesting.

“The information submitted does not include a site report that demonstrates that the location of the proposed facility complies with all applicable siting requirements and that the site is a suitable location for the proposed facility as required by [state siting rules],” wrote Michael Wimsatt, director of DES’s Waste Management Division.

In addition, the maps, figures, and hydro-geological report initially submitted as part of the site report in October 2023 “were not updated to demonstrate compliance with the re-adopted Part Env-Sw 804, Siting Requirements, of the Rules, which became effective on December 21, 2024,” said Wimsatt.

“The information submitted also does not include copies of the documents that demonstrate that the applicant and their successors and assigns will have a legal right for the use of the properties as proposed in the application as required by [state rules],” he said.

In addition to missing information, Casella has continued to black out other information, despite DES’s February 2024 incompleteness determination letter telling the company that the redacted legal agreements as submitted are preventing DES review.

“Each of the subsequent incompleteness determination letters reiterated that copies of the legal agreements as required by Env-Sw 314.03 needed to be provided,” said Wimsatt. “The legal agreements submitted by the applicant failed to fulfill application requirements because they were heavily redacted and referenced other legal agreements, which were not provided.”

For instance, the access agreement and 2018 option to purchase real estate was heavily redacted and these redactions rendered DES unable to fulfill permit application requirements, said Wimsatt.

Similarly, Casella submitted a memorandum of understanding that was undated and unsigned. A signed version was later submitted, but does not fulfill application requirements because it was also heavily redacted and relies on the terms of the option agreement and 2019 amendments to the agreement, with the amendments referenced in the application, but not provided.

DES issued letters to Casella deeming the application incomplete on Feb. 28, 2024; June 24, 2024; Oct. 22, 2024; and Jan. 27, 2025. The letters also provided notice of the dormancy deadline.

Under state rules, the permit application was denied because Casella did not submit the information necessary to complete the application within the required year of DES’s initial determination of incompleteness.
Casella has 30 days to file an appeal.

Meanwhile, the company had a deadline of March 24 to file permit applications for its wetlands and alteration of terrain permits, both of which were also the subject of multiple letters of incompleteness by DES.
According to a check on Friday afternoon, neither revised application to meet the 2025 deadline had been posted to DES’s landfill permitting page.

“Our Granite State Landfill website is up to date,” said James Martin, spokesman for DES.

Lawmaker, Residents, Property Owner Weigh In

Among those buoyed by DES’s denial was state Sen. David Rochefort, R-Littleton, who said his phone has been buzzing since DES handed down its decision.

“For a rainy, cloudy day, the sun is shining,” he said Thursday. “This is great. This has been a longtime effort. A cast of characters from all walks of life have really worked hard on this. It really feels surreal, actually.”
He said that opposition to the landfill has been a big issue for the North Country.

“As I was campaigning all over the district — a third of the state — it was not a NIMBY issue, despite what some people say,” said Rochefort. “This was an issue of the whole region, and the whole state. People value clean water, they value the character of the place where we live, and a lot of people fought long and hard, before I even came to the fight. It’s very satisfying and very encouraging to see this.”

Adam Finkel, an environmental scientist and Dalton resident, said he was struck by DES’s “sudden change after six years of letters that were so deferential and so permissive.”

“This was like, ‘What don’t you understand about enough is enough?’” he said. “And I feel that they’re finally taking the proper tone that an agency should take with an applicant.”

Finkel said he understands Casella’s dilemma about not wanting to buy property without a permit.

“But they should have realized at the outset that the law doesn’t let them do that,” he said. “They tried to do this catch-22 thing and get the deed and the permit simultaneously and it just doesn’t work like that.”

To deny an application based on easements and ownerships is great, but the flaws of the application went well beyond that because Casella chose a poor site beside a lake and on sand and gravel with fast-moving groundwater beneath, said Finkel.

“You owe it to the department and the people to actually figure out where the pollution’s going to go when it leaks, not if it leaks, and you refused to do that,” he said. “You should have been turned away months into this farce. [DES] should have said, ‘Come back after you’ve looked at the site. Don’t waste our time.’ All of this was avoidable.”

Dalton resident Jon Swan, who founded Save Forest Lake to oppose the landfill, was also cautiously optimistic after DES’s decision.

“We have had to do the work that DES should have been doing all along and we’re doing the work now relative to oversight,” he said.

“We want to commend DES for doing the right thing and denying this unneeded and unwanted project,” said Tom Tower, vice-president of the North Country Alliance for Balanced Change. “Although this news is encouraging, we are still laser focused on supporting some great legislation led by Gov. Ayotte and the New Hampshire House that will help make sure no community has to go through what the North Country has endured over the last four years.”

On Friday afternoon, Casella spokesman Jeff Weld said “We have received the notice from NHDES and we are currently considering our options.”

On Friday, property owner Douglas Ingerson Jr., of Dalton, was asked if he’s hopeful the landfill will go through and if he’ll be able to sell pieces of his property to Casella.

“I could give a f*** less,” said Ingerson, who owns Chick’s Sand and Gravel. “I’ve done well my whole life. I don’t need a landfill. I’m going to work on my drag strip. There’s no zoning in my town. The drag strip goes forward. I’ve already started the drag strip. We already have almost 200 feet down. This year, we’ll probably get almost 600 down.”

He said he could sell to Casella, but doesn’t have to if the landfill doesn’t move forward.

“I’ve got my concrete business, have an industrial park, I’ve got the drag strip, I sell lumber, I do jobs,” said Ingerson. “I’ve done it my whole life. It’s one more thing. No biggie if it don’t come. I don’t care too much about it.”

It’s up to Casella on whether it wants to submit another application, he said.
“I just sit back and watch the idiots argue over the trash they don’t know what to do with,” said Ingerson. “That’s my opinion of the whole thing. Everybody’s got their issue. I’m not upset about it.”

Ayotte and lawmalers agree on a three-year landfill moratorium in New Hampshire. Is this the final answer? 

 
New Hampshire is inching closer to a pause on new landfills, with Gov. Kelly Ayotte now supporting a three-year moratorium — a middle ground between her first proposals and the five-to-six year ban lawmakers sought.

In February, Ayotte endorsed a one-year moratorium on new landfill development. The amended policy section of the state budget, known as HB2, includes a three-year moratorium on new landfills.

State Rep. Nicholas Germana, a key advocate for landfill legislation, sees the three-year moratorium as reasonable.

“It’s clearly a compromise between what some of us would like to see, five or six and what the governor had originally supported. Three falls in the middle,” said Germana, a Keene Democrat. “So I think that that hopefully will be a strong sign to the Senate that this is the direction we ought to go in.”

Originally, the House backed a five-year moratorium before settling on three. The Senate had considered a six-year pause, but it was killed.

If approved, the three-year moratorium would last until July 1, 2028.

During the pause, the Department of Environmental Services would still be able to accept new landfill applications, but it will only determine if they’re complete.

In addition, the trailer bill puts a temporary hold on any new solid waste disposal capacity — meaning no new landfills, large transfer stations, collection or storage facilities would be approved until new rules are adopted or by July 1, 2026, whichever comes first.

The push on the brakes comes from environmental advocates and lawmakers who want to give the state more time to strengthen landfill regulations, arguing that the solid waste rules updated in December are too weak.

One concern is that the new rules would allow landfills to be built in locations that could threaten groundwater, surface water and public health.

Michael Wimsatt, director of the state’s environmental agency, declined to take a stance on the latest version of the bill.

“What I can tell you is that the original version of HB2, as presented to the House by the governor, we support,” said Wimsatt.

Lawmakers on the House Environment and Agriculture Committee struck another deal on landfill legislation — this time, focusing on the impact to a host community. That means weighing everything from human health risks and property values to water pollution, greenhouse gas emissions, tourism, outdoor recreation and affect on wildlife.

Originally, the trailer bill placed this assessment at the very end of the permitting process.

After working with the Governor’s office, Germana said they’ve reached a compromise — the assessment will happen at the beginning for any new proposals, before permits are even considered. For landfill applications already in progress, like Casella Waste Systems’ controversial Dalton proposal, the assessment will still happen at the end.

Every year, lawmakers introduce several bills aimed at strengthening landfill regulations in the state, which imports as much as half of all trash from neighboring states. The state’s environmental agency has stayed neutral on landfill legislation and often points to concerns about how tighter rules could affect the solid waste market in New Hampshire. These bills rarely make it to the governor’s desk.

Germana thinks this year could be different.

“The folks at DES, I’m confident, are always trying to do what’s right for the environment, but they’re also operating in a political environment, and their directives come from the governor’s office,” said Germana. “So under Gov. Sununu, we had a governor who was more interested in corporate interests, and in this case, I want to give Gov. Ayotte credit for being much more concerned about public health and the environment.”

N.H. House Passes Moratorium Bill, Resolution Opposing Landfill
by Robert Blechl March 21, 2025 - Posted with Permission

In a Thursday morning floor vote, the New Hampshire House of Representatives adopted on consent a resolution opposing a landfill beside Forest Lake State Park and passed a 3-year moratorium on the permitting of new landfills.

House Resolution 13, sponsored by state Rep. Jared Sullivan, D-Bethlehem, and local co-sponsors, including state Reps. Seth King, R-Whitefield, Joseph Barton, R-Littleton, and Lori Korzen, R-Berlin, “urges the New Hampshire Department of Environmental Services to deny permitting a landfill next to Forest Lake State Park in Dalton.”

Sullivan addressed his 400 House colleagues at the close of Thursday’s 8 1/2-hour session.  

(Link:  https://youtu.be/oYMo7COZZ6Y )

“I would like to thank the relevant committee and the wider body for passing HR 13,” he said.

In reading the language of the resolution, Sullivan said the 397-acre park with a 200-foot sandy beach is one of New Hampshire’s original state parks, was created in 1935, hosts popular activities such as swimming, picnicking, mountain biking, fishing, and boating, and an operating landfill would create nuisances and public health and environmental threats, would hurt the North Country tourism and outdoor recreation economies, and “the risks in permitting a landfill next to Forest Lake State Park far outweigh any benefit.”

“Thank you all for supporting this on the consent calendar a couple of weeks ago,” Sullivan said to the House members, who broke into applause.

Although non-binding, supporters of the resolution have said its sends a strong message and makes the House’s position clear.
House Bill 171, also sponsored by local lawmakers and which now moves to the New Hampshire Senate, seeks to impose a moratorium on the permitting of new landfills until July 2028, though NHDES can still accept applications for review.
Before an amendment to the bill, the proposed moratorium expiration date had been July 2030.

Proponents say the pause would give the state more time to better assess the need, benefit, harm, or appropriateness of a proposed site and give time to adopt new siting regulations and explore new solid waste management or technologies.
The moratorium would not prohibit the expansion of existing landfills.

As residents closely watch landfill-related bills, Senate Bill 227, sponsored by state Rep. David Rochefort, R-Littleton, and co-sponsored by King, seeks a site-specific setback for landfills from surface waters based on how fast the groundwater moves beneath a landfill, with the intent to have enough time if a leak or spill contaminates groundwater.

According to SB 227, a period of five years should be sufficient to detect and map a failure and assess the appropriate remediation before contaminants reach rivers, lakes, or other surface waters.

While the full House has supported landfill bills in recent years, they’ve often encountered defeat in the Senate.
On March 6, state Sen. Kevin Avard, R-Nashua, made a successful motion in the Senate’s Energy and Natural Resources Committee, which he chairs, to table SB 227.

On Tuesday in the House’s Environment and Agriculture Committee, the committee gave SB 227’s counterpart bill, House Bill 707, a 14-1 ought-to-pass-as-amended vote.

HB 707 could go to a full House vote next week before moving to the Senate. Other bills also remain pending.

Although Casella Waste Systems, which proposes the commercial Granite State Landfill LLC (GSL) in Dalton, hasn’t found a supportive lawmaker in the North Country, or at least publicly, it has found a receptive audience downstate in Avard.

On Dec. 13, on the eve of a Joint Legislative Committee on Administrative rules session to consider new solid waste management and landfill rules, Avard interviewed Casella CEO John Casella for a 28-minute segment on Gateway City Chronicles.  (Link: https://youtu.be/uMpQbO1VSpc )

According to the video, Avard and Casella suggested that the Commerce Clause of the U.S. Constitution cannot prohibit out-of-state waste from coming into New Hampshire, although some lawmakers and North Country residents have previously said that surrounding states have been successful in reducing or eliminating the amount of interstate trash that comes across their borders.

Avard referred to the landfill opposition as a NIMBY (“not in my backyard”) issue and said to Casella, “I think we have a bright future if we can just get past a lot of the naysayers out there.”

Among those residents watching the Gate City Chronicles interview was Adam Finkel, of Dalton.

“Calling experts who know more about the risks of pollution than you do ‘NIMBYs’ is the last refuge of amateur CEOs who perhaps should find another business to be in,” Finkel said Thursday. “The opposition to the GSL is so overwhelming because the scientific and economic case for it is so abysmally weak.”�

https://www.caledonianrecord.com/news/local/n-h-house-passes-moratorium-bill-resolution-opposing-landfill/article_f2236e2b-e469-56f3-86b5-619fc5e05825.html

Will Pennsylvania crack down on local governments for restricting fracking? 

Last fall, Cecil Township, a suburb of Pittsburgh in Washington County, Pennsylvania, voted to increase the minimum setback distance of fracking wells from 500 feet to 2,500 feet away from homes and businesses.   

Three days later, the state’s Republican Senators introduced legislation that would penalize communities like Cecil Township for taking action like this to protect residents from the pollution and noise of shale gas drilling.   

Senate Bill 1346 would withhold funds meant to offset the impacts of oil and gas drilling from municipalities that pass increased setback ordinances. These “impact fees” are collected from the fracking industry and typically go towards improvements to roads and other infrastructure, emergency preparedness, and environmental protection programs.   

Lois Bower-Bjornson, Southwestern Pennsylvania Field Organizer with Clean Air Council, said the bill is clearly in retaliation for Cecil Township’s ordinance.
   
“The lawmakers say Cecil Township is stopping fracking,” she said. “But they aren’t. These are simply protective measures to protect community members.” 

State Senator Camera Bartolotta, a sponsor of the bill, said the impact fees “were never intended for communities that actively block the responsible use of Pennsylvania's natural resources.”

The following email was received by all providers around 9:30 this morning: 
Today, the Indiana Family and Social Services Administration's Office of Early Childhood and Out-of-School Learning (OECOSL) announced revised Child Care and Development Fund (CCDF) voucher reimbursement rates. These adjustments address a $225 million funding gap through 2026 created by the prior administration's unsustainable use of temporary COVID relief funds and ensure continued compliance with federal requirements.

To establish equitable rates, OECOSL received surveys from 25 percent of licensed child care providers and analyzed their cost data, arriving at reimbursement levels that reflect current operating realities.

"We made this decision to protect the children and families that depend on CCDF vouchers. There is only one pot of money – we could either protect providers or kids, and we chose kids," said Adam Alson, Director of OECOSL.

As of July, approximately 55,000 Hoosier children rely on CCDF vouchers—a decrease from the December 2024 peak of 68,000, yet still markedly above the pre-pandemic average of 35,000 recipients.

These rate changes prevent disruptions to voucher access and maintain federal compliance by establishing sustainable rates:

Infants (0–12 months): 10 percent decrease
Toddlers (13–36 months): 10 percent decrease
Preschoolers (3–5 years): 15 percent decrease
School-Age Children (K–12): 35 percent decrease

BIG WIN for clean water in the Triangle area of North Carolina.  

@[100069574183939:2048:Sound Rivers] and SELC have reached a settlement with developer Mungo Homes after years of illegal sediment pollution turned local streams orange and threatened Raleigh’s drinking water supply. 

The agreement forces the developer to: 

- Take actions to prevent ongoing pollution into Martin Branch & Hurricane Creek 

- Fund protection of 62 acres in the Lick Creek watershed 

- Pay civil penalties and attorney fees 

This sends a clear message: if you pollute our waterways in the South, you’ll have to face SELC and our local partners. 

“By compelling this developer to contribute to land conservation in the Lick Creek watershed, we are able to turn this egregious environmental harm into a lasting protection for this impaired waterway and the community members who rely on it.” - Samantha Krop, Sound Rivers’ Neuse Riverkeeper 

Learn more: https://www.selc.org/news/a-win-for-water-quality-in-north-carolina/

We talk a lot about reporting and preventing water pollution, but many people don’t know what falls into that category! Here are 6 common sources of water pollution in our area. Do your part to prevent these things from going into storm drains and report pollutants to 3-1-1 if you see them!

Ask Cambridge to End Sewage Pollution by 2050. 

The Health and Environment Committee of the Cambridge City Council will meet on Tuesday afternoon, from 2 pm to 4 pm, on September 9th. They will discuss the city’s Sewage Pollution Plans. For Alewife Brook, a meaningful plan means a 25-year level of control* for Cambridge’s Combined Sewer Overflows (CSOs). Cambridge’s CSOs dump the most raw sewage into Alewife Brook during rain storms.

You can help! Please send a written comment here: https://savethealewifebrook.org/2025/09/06/ask-cambridge-to-finish-the-job-end-sewage-pollution-by-2050/#emailhealthenvironment 

Or come to the meeting in person or via streaming.
https://www.cambridgema.gov/citycalendar/view.aspx?guid=96e52447763048e4950ef19b8d9b39a1

You can also email them directly - be sure to email the City Clerk, too:
CityCouncil@Cambridgema.gov CityClerk@cambridgema.gov 

Explain why ending sewage pollution is important to you. Feel free to tell the Council your personal story. Ask the Council for action to End to Sewage Pollution by 2050. 

Ask them for a plan that includes a combination of:

Complete Sewer Separation by 2050.
Green Stormwater Infrastructure.
Underground Combined Sewage Storage Tanks.
Engineer a 25-year level of control for the year 2050*

* A 25-year level of control is what engineers often refer to as “virtual” combined sewage elimination. It means that there are no sewage discharges, except in a really big storm that occurs roughly every 25 years. Today’s “25-year storm” has a 4% chance of occurring in any given year, statistically expected once every 25 years. That means around 6 inches of rain in 24-hours, according to the most current rainfall model (NOAA Atlas 14).

The increase in global surface temperatures will surpass the 2 °C target set by the Paris Agreement unless carbon emissions are lowered to zero by 2050. To date, the building sector is responsible for 38% of all carbon emissions, thus one of the main targets is represented by the development of building strategies that can facilitate the transition toward carbon-neutral buildings.,Construction is a major source of carbon emissions. Moreover, it faces various other sustainability challenges, such as construction waste, construction noise, vehicular traffic near construction sites, dust and other air and water pollutants, and safety and well-being of construction workers. Poorly designed and constructed buildings will continue to affect the well-being of their occupants and overall energy efficiency throughout the building lifecycle.,Due to its similarity to industrialized products, the offsite construction industry is seen as a focus for the transformation of Construction 4.0. Many digital technologies have been applied or have the potential to be applied to realize the integration of design, manufacturing, and assembly.,Soft skills are essential to employability and retention; therefore, if obtained and observed, they can significantly reduce sector-wide turnover. This study aims to investigate and compare soft skills that industry professionals currently possess and soft skills the industry requires and needs to attain.,The labor-intensive nature of the construction industry requires workers to frequently perform physically demanding manual work, thereby exposing them to the risk of musculoskeletal injury (approximately 31.2 cases per 10,000 full-time equivalent workers). Exoskeletons and exosuits (collectively called EXOs here) are designed to protect workers from these injuries by reducing exertion and muscle fatigue during work.,Green buildings (GBs) play an important role in achieving sustainable development goals. However, the implementation of green technologies in building projects has not reached the desired level in developing countries such as Turkey because of barriers stemming from country-related factors as well as factors related to design and construction. The objective of this study was to explore the barriers to undertaking GB projects in Turkey.,Off-site construction (OSC) is an innovative construction method that transfers most of the site-based work to a more controlled environment. Construction waste minimization, speedy schedules, higher sustainability, and better quality are some of the perceived benefits of OSC. Therefore, significant research attention has been given to OSC.,The integration of Design for Manufacture and Assembly (DfMA) into the design process of industrialized construction has the potential to reduce errors and changes occurring after the design has been finalized, ultimately improving overall productivity. Based on DfMA, the designers would need to consider whether their designs meet the architectural and performance requirements, as well as the manufacturing and assembly requirements from assembly and manufacturing technicians.

Sustainable materials matter when building a home – after all, homes are investments, which means so are the materials they are made of! Here's why sustainable materials make a difference:

🌱 Environmental Impact: Opting for eco-friendly materials reduces your home's carbon footprint and preserves natural resources for future generations.

🌱 Long-Term Savings: Sustainable materials often boast superior durability and energy efficiency, saving you money on maintenance and utility bills over time.

🌱 Healthier Living Spaces: Non-toxic, low-emission materials contribute to better indoor air quality, creating a healthier and more comfortable environment for you and your loved ones.

We don't just want to build you a home to-go, we want to build you a forever home that lasts!

Eco-friendly building materials are making waves in sustainable construction, offering homeowners both environmental benefits and beautiful design options. 

Sustainable wood, sourced from responsibly managed forests, is a prime choice for construction and furniture. It not only reduces deforestation but also adds natural warmth and character to any space.

Recycled materials are also gaining popularity. Recycled steel and plastic are being repurposed into durable building materials, helping to reduce waste and lower the carbon footprint of new homes.

Natural insulation materials like wool, cork, and cellulose provide excellent energy efficiency while avoiding the harmful chemicals found in conventional insulation. These materials are biodegradable and often locally sourced, making them a smart choice for eco-conscious homeowners.

Green roofing options are another fantastic way to enhance sustainability. Living roofs, covered in vegetation, help absorb rainwater and reduce heat, while cool roofs reflect sunlight to lower energy costs and reduce urban heat islands. Both options contribute to a greener, more energy-efficient home.

ad_creative_bodies:
June is Pollinator Awareness Month! 🦋 Pollinators are essential to the health of our ecosystems, yet they face increasing threats from habitat loss, pollution, pesticides, land development, and climate change. Their populations are declining, but you can make a difference!

By planting pollinator-friendly native habitat, you create a safe haven for pollinators in your own landscape. 

Pictured below: Pollinators thriving at Moonlight Farm—a National Wildlife Federation certified habitat and a registered pit stop on The Butterfly Highway!

Did you know? Pollinators don’t just help plants grow—they sustain wildlife by supporting fruits, seeds, and vegetation that countless species rely on, from birds and mammals to insects and reptiles.

Learn more about North Carolina’s pollinators and how you can help:  https://ncwf.org/blog/birds-bees-pollinators/

page_name: Indiana Renewable Energy by EDPR NA
ad_creative_bodies:
🐛🦋 Pollinators like bees, butterflies, birds, and bats may be some of our smallest-statured neighbors, but they’re a big deal!  

Pollinators are responsible for sustaining an estimated 75% of flowering plants and 35% of food crops. As many pollinator species are declining, it’s more vital than ever to know how to help them bounce back. The best way is to do your part to give them a home. 🌼 

Vegetation management practices on many utility-scale renewable energy project sites include the establishment of pollinator habitat with seed mixes chosen alongside local experts. In your own yard or garden, you can choose to landscape using plants and features that support the pollinating animals in your ecosystem. 🦇🐝 

Plug in your zip code and find your area’s Selecting Plants for Pollinators Guide with this tool from @[100064752608776:2048:The Pollinator Partnership]:

Let’s take a moment to celebrate National Pollinator Week, which spans the last full week in June each year! 🐝 

Bees are all the buzz this summer at our operational solar farming project in Madison County, Madison Fields Solar Project. We are so excited to be working with London’s own local Dr. John Boeckl, engineer by trade and beekeeper by passion, who will be caring for the hives on site.  Pollinators play a vital role in our ecosystems, helping contribute to a cleaner, more sustainable future – just like solar energy.

We’re celebrating Pollinator Week by highlighting the indispensable work of our state’s bees, butterflies, and more that keep our home beautiful and flourishing. 🐝 

Many of our solar projects act as hubs for these critical critters by incorporating pollinator-supporting grasses, shrubs, and wildflowers on-site, which serve as habitats for pollinators and other prairie-dwelling wildlife. 

📸 Stephanie Lein, captured near Darien Solar Energy Center

Did you know that pollinators and bees are installed at solar farms to help populate the pollinators? It's World Honey Bee Day - do you know any beekeepers? If so, thank them for contributing to our pollinator population!

As we close out 2024, the message is clear: the systems we’ve built—our infrastructure, energy, and economies—are increasingly vulnerable to a changing climate. This year brought record-breaking heat, disruptive weather, and undeniable signals that adaptation and action can no longer wait.

But amidst the challenges, there’s progress. Investments in renewable energy are growing, innovations in energy storage and industrial efficiency are taking hold, and public demand for systemic change is louder than ever. These aren’t just trends; they’re building blocks for a more resilient and equitable future.

2025 is an opportunity to take the momentum of this year and push for policies, innovations, and accountability that prioritize long-term solutions over short-term gains. The future isn’t just about surviving change—it’s about thriving through it.

Here’s to action, innovation, and a shared commitment to a better future.

✨ In Costa Rica’s Playa Ostional, one of nature’s greatest spectacles unfolds: the arribada. For a few magical days, tens of thousands of Olive Ridley sea turtles emerge from the ocean together to nest. 🐢

🌍 This rare phenomenon only happens in a handful of places on Earth — Costa Rica, Mexico & India — and Ostional is one of the largest, most important nesting sites. Each turtle lays nearly 100 eggs, continuing a cycle of life that’s been happening for generations.

💚 Protecting this magic matters. Rising pollution, plastics, and climate change threaten these turtles — but change begins right where we are. Start small: pick up trash, recycle, reduce single-use plastics. Every act of care ripples outward.

🌊 And for us, it’s even more special — because this is home. The place where we built Mindful Waves, and where we’re reminded daily that people and nature can still live in harmony. ✨

DYK: Amphibians like frogs, toads and salamanders come large and small, brightly colored or well-camouflaged, frilled, spotted, and every other variation you can think of, and many are right here in our own backyards and woods. 🐸

Sadly, however, many species are threatened or endangered as their habitats become smaller due to increased development and pollution. And now, these animals may be facing what experts are calling an “amphibian apocalypse."

That’s why we’re working across the country to prevent the destruction of wetlands and other amphibian habitats.

Learn more about amphibians!

In just 70 years, over 130,000 breeding pairs of African penguins have disappeared, leaving the species on the brink of extinction. 🇿🇦

Just 3% of the original population remains, and every year, these dwindling numbers are reduced even further, thanks to overfishing and rampant pollution. 

Off the coast of South Africa, in Algoa Bay, around 700 breeding pairs are struggling to survive in a vital colony. 

But as potential parents die from starvation, their eggs are left abandoned and at risk. 

Our partner, the Southern African Foundation for the Conservation of Coastal Birds (SANCCOB), runs a crucial program to save these eggs, incubating and hand-rearing chicks until they’re ready to be released into protected areas. 

As countless eggs are in danger, they need all the help they can get. 

Every rescued egg is essential to the survival of the entire species, and we must help them save as many as possible. 

🔗 Take action today to save the African penguins: https://bit.ly/ASISPR03F.

Photo credit: Tom Darby, Joshua Kettle, & SAAMBR.

This human-made crisis has also left deep scars on the natural world. Once a stopover for thousands of birds migrating between Europe and Asia or Africa, the wetlands around Aden have become a shadow of their former selves.

“The world is round, the sun rises in the east, coal- and gas-fired power plants contribute significantly to climate change, and climate change increases the risk of heat waves, catastrophic storms and many other health threats...These are indisputable facts. If you torpedo regulations on power plant greenhouse gas emissions, you torpedo the health and well-being of the American public and contribute to leaving a world of risk and suffering to our children and grandchildren.”

That's the view of a scientist quoted on greenhouse gas emissions in this article. The view of the administration - which has decided to roll back regulations on CO2 emissions from power plants as well as toxins such as mercury - can be read here:

In its proposed new regulations, the Trump EPA argues that carbon dioxide and other greenhouse gases from fossil fuel-fired power plants “do not contribute significantly to dangerous pollution” or climate change and therefore do not meet a threshold under the Clean Air Act for regulatory action. Greenhouse gas emissions from coal and gas-fired plants “are a small and decreasing part of global emissions,’' the EPA said, adding: “This Administration’s priority is to promote the public health or welfare through energy dominance and independence secured by using fossil fuels to generate power.”

Two different views, and both sides cannot be right. In terms of total CO2 emissions in the U.S., power plants produce about 25%, second only to transportation (27%).

“I’m Steven Donziger, here with a lot of gratitude and an update on my pardon campaign. 

For those of you just joining, I’m a human rights lawyer who in New York has been helping Amazon Indigenous communities fight Chevron Texaco for 30 years over massive environmental destruction the company caused in Ecuador. Although we secured a landmark $10 billion judgment for the Indigenous peoples, Chevron has never paid even the first dollar. 

Instead, they’ve come after me in a vicious corporate retaliation campaign. They had me locked up at home without trial for over two years, took my passport, and stripped me of my law license without a hearing. 

But I have not stopped fighting. And thanks to your tremendous support, I’m happy to say we’ve built a lot of momentum in the past few months. 

This summer, over 50 civil society petitioned President Biden for my pardon, including Amnesty International, Greenpeace, Amazon Watch, and Global Witness. I’m so grateful to these environmental and human rights organizations who are standing with me and the Amazon communities. 

In mid-June we went directly to the White House. We rallied with so many of you and Rep Cori Bush, Rep Jim McGovern, and Rep Rashida Tlaid as well as friends from Amnesty, Amazon Watch, and Climate Defiance. I was deeply heartened by the support at our rally and also overseas where many of you protested outside of American embassies all over the world. 

We’ve also raised almost $250,000 in the last six months. This is no small feat and is made up of thousands of small donations from people like you who are just as mad as I am at Big Oil. These funds have allowed me to survive by paying legal fees in dealing with Chevron’s attacks. The funds also allow us to build a small staff to support the pardon campaign. 

President Biden is leaving office in January. That’s just a few months away. This is our time to act. We hope to reach those closest to Biden to help secure a pardon. We’re doing as much education as we can and hope to run an ad in leading newspapers to build support and get his attention. 

One of the best parts of this campaign has been meeting people who believe as strongly as I do that corporations should not be allowed to destroy our environment to artificially raise their profits to record levels, as Chevron did in Ecuador. 

Whatever you can do to support our campaign, whether it’s sharing with friends or donating directly, I appreciate your support so much. You can find the full details of my story and ways to donate on my chuffed campaign page. 

Steven Donziger”

“When I first arrived in the Amazon as an idealistic, young man fresh out of law school, what I saw made me outraged. I had heard about evil corporations prioritizing profit for the few over healthy and livable environments for the many. But seeing it first hand was deeply disillusioning. Truly shocking. 

Literal pools of oil everywhere. Oil on people’s skin and clothes, in the water and air, their health, declining rapidly. Such an incredible place of natural beauty and Indigenous lifeways was being systematically poisoned without a second thought by Big Oil. 

I knew I could not turn my back on the people I had met. I resolved then and there to come back as soon as I could with more resources. By the mid 90’s, I had helped put together a team of lawyers both in Ecuador and New York to do battle against Chevron. 

So many of my peers, employed in their first gigs in big law firms, laughed at me. Chevron was one of the most powerful companies in the world. It had a track record of spending millions to attack anyone who dared to challenge it. Asking for billions of dollars of damages from Big Oil for Indigenous peoples and farmers? It seemed like an impossible fight. 

But somehow, with a great team of lawyers in Ecuador and the support of so many around the world, we won. 

We secured a $20 billion judgment in trial, later reduced to just under $10 billion on appeal. It was and still is the biggest environmental judgment ever awarded by a court of law. What a thrill to have gone up against Goliath and have won a truly impactful result for vulnerable peoples who deserved and needed this help! 

But, of course, Chevron has refused to pay. 

Instead, they've come after me with a vicious demonization campaign. The goal is to drive me off the case. Even destroy my ability to work or practice law. And use the example to  send a message of intimidation to other climate activists that anybody who messes with Big Oil will be destroyed. 

The amount of corporate vengeance they’ve directed my way is staggering. They’ve got courts who do their bidding, they’ve thrown money around, they’ve coached witnesses to present false testimony. They’ve used 60 law firms and 2,000 lawyers to attack me and our team.

And to some degree it’s worked - Chevron had me locked up at home and in prison for nearly 3 years. They took my passport, my bank accounts, even my law license. 

I'm refusing to give up.  

I won't let Big Oil silence me or our movement, and I won't disappoint that idealistic young lawyer in these photos. Even after all these years, I still feel the same fire and passion for the Amazon communities and the same rage at Chevron for the destruction and pain they've caused, all in the name of profits. 

Can you help me? 

I have appealed the bogus convictions they leveled at me, but the only realistic way for me to continue this extraordinary fight is to get a pardon from President Biden. He announced he won’t be seeking a second term, so our time is short. 

We only have until January. 

Your donation will help me increase the pressure of my pardon campaign this fall. That will include a direct education campaign with White House officials -- and hopefully a full-page ad in the leading US newspapers. We’ve got to get President Biden’s attention before he leaves office in January. We’ve got to make him understand that thousands of people around the world support the communities of Ecuador and are watching.

Big Oil cannot silence me. They cannot silence the affected communities in Ecuador. They cannot silence our climate movement. We will keep fighting for the environment and holding these money-hungry corporations accountable for the destruction they’ve caused. 

Thank you for your help! 

Steven Donziger”

Honesty and Facts Over Misinformation and Politics

I was raised to believe in honesty and transparency.  As First Selectman, I will always be straightforward with Easton residents.  There have been a lot of passions and misinformation circulating online regarding a proposed high-density development on Plum Tree Lane in both Easton and Trumbull consisting of seventy units.  I believe in educating voters with clear, factual updates about this proposal and how this process works—not stoking fear among residents for political gain.

My Position on Plum Tree:

I am strongly against high-density development of any kind in Easton.  I am firmly against this proposed development.  Let me be clear that when I say that I am for ‘Preservation with Progress,’ progress does NOT mean residential or commercial development of any kind.  ‘Progress’ means a renovated EMS building, upgraded radios for our emergency services, improved recreational facilities (i.e. pickle ball courts), an expanded library, and continued investment in our schools’ infrastructure.  As First Selectman, I would use all the powers at my disposal to fight this or any similar project.

Proposed Project’s Current Status:

The Plum Tree project is far from final.  It will require approval for sewer hookup by the Water Pollution Control Authorities of both Trumbull and Bridgeport.  It will then need approval by the Inland Wetlands boards in both Easton and Trumbull.  Additionally, the Planning and Zoning Commission in both Easton and Trumbull would have to approve the project.  Finally, the project would likely face challenges in Connecticut Superior Court.  In short, there is a lengthy legal and regulatory process still ahead.

Explaining Connecticut’s 8-30g Housing Statute:

This proposal is being pursued under Connecticut’s 8-30g affordable housing statute. Under 8-30g, developers may override certain local zoning regulations in towns where less than 10% of housing qualifies as affordable.  It is my belief that Easton—and similar towns—should be exempt due to our size, rural character, and watershed status.  However, that is not the case until we can successfully change state law.

It is important to understand the challenge 8-30g creates for towns like ours.  Planning & Zoning can only deny an 8-30g project on very narrow grounds—specifically, if the development creates substantial health or safety risks that clearly outweigh the need for affordable housing, and if those concerns are backed by evidence.  General concerns about density, traffic, school impacts, or neighborhood character are not enough.  This makes legal appeals costly and difficult, but not impossible when the facts support the town’s case.

Role of First Selectman in Planning and Zoning Decisions:

It is also critical to clarify what a First Selectman can and cannot do. By law, the First Selectman cannot control the decisions of the Planning & Zoning Commission.  In Easton, the Planning & Zoning Commission is an independent body, and its decisions are final unless appealed to Superior Court by directly affected parties. Neither the First Selectman nor the Board of Selectmen can substitute their judgment for that of P&Z. Any claims to the contrary are misleading the public.  This is one of the many reasons that I strongly and successfully advocated for P&Z to become an elected position.  Easton residents should directly choose these representatives.

Democrats’ Record on Preservation:

Let me also highlight the Bindelglass-D’Addario record on preservation.  For years, some have claimed that Democrats want to “develop Easton.”  The facts show otherwise. In the last six years, we passed a land use ordinance that gave residents — not the Board of Selectmen — final control over the acquisition and sale of town land.  Alongside the Aspetuck Land Trust and the State of Connecticut, we preserved major properties such as South Park (now Kupinse Open Space), Slady Farm, Poindexter, and Lantern Hill.  We reconstituted the Land Acquisition Authority.  As a member of the Planning and Zoning Commission, I voted for regulations to provide a conservation development option for builders that would preserve more open space and offer an alternative to 8-30g applications in Easton.  I also voted to allow accessory use dwellings—in particular the option to deed them affordable which helps Easton work towards an 8-30g moratorium.  Recently, when a statewide housing bill threatened to override local authority, our direct conversations with the Governor and outreach to his office helped secure his veto.  Our approach is action, not rhetoric: preserving land, working with partners, and protecting Easton’s rural character.

My Promise to Easton Residents:

I promise to fight high-density development like the proposed 8-30g Plum Tree project.  However, I will not lie to voters and say that as First Selectman I can stop anything from being built on Plum Tree or any other property.  Frankly, it is not in the powers of the First Selectman or the Board of Selectmen to do so.  Easton can and should make it as difficult and expensive as possible for any developer to try to build high-density development using the 8-30g statute.  I promise that I will fight like hell to ensure that we protect and preserve open space and ensure that Easton’s rural character remains intact.  Further, I will always be straightforward with Easton residents.

Nick D’Addario

Lauren Davis: "Did you know that the White House has announced new protections for 13 million acres of the Western Arctic in Alaska, safeguarding one of the nation’s most beautiful and pristine wilderness areas in the world?

Last year I took a trip to Juneau, Alaska and was completely captivated by all of the beauty Alaska has to offer. While I’ve yet to explore the Arctic firsthand, I can only imagine its breathtaking beauty, and hearing about the dedication to protect this pristine region fills me with hope. Protecting lands and waters from oil and gas development will help reduce greenhouse gas emissions and lessen the impact of climate change. These actions not only prioritize wildlife and local communities, but also uphold America’s commitment to environmental stewardship.

Let’s celebrate this milestone in protecting one of our planet’s most precious treasures."

The Western Arctic has some of the most pristine wildlands in the U.S., where several species—spanning from beluga whales to caribou to even polar bears—rely on the rich resources from this area for their survival. It’s a vast ecological treasure that’s also home to dozens of Indigenous communities. 

In a recent victory, the Biden administration put 10 million acres off-limits to oil and gas leasing across the Western Arctic. However, the work does not stop there. The administration will be accepting public comments on a new plan—one that will aim to protect even more Arctic lands from the exploitative and dangerous practices of the oil industry. Help protect the Arctic: https://action.nrdc.org/letter/3235-special-areas-061624?initms=FBONASAPPETC30624&ms=FBONASAPPETC30624&utm_medium=social&utm_source=facebook&utm_campaign=federal

The Senate tried to sell our public lands. Now, they are trying to sell off our ocean. Take Action 📣 

The massive budget reconciliation bill, working its way through the Senate, is proposing to sell leases on over 80 million acres of the ocean for new offshore oil and gas drilling — and that’s just in year one. Over the 15-year timeframe, 1.2 BILLION acres of our ocean could be exploited. The bill would also reduce environmental protections while increasing taxpayer-funded federal subsidies to drill off U.S. coasts. It’s an enormous corporate giveaway to the fossil fuel industry that’s already reporting record profits, at the expense of our ocean environment, our climate, and our health.

Every oil spill starts with a lease sale. And every new lease sale locks the U.S. into decades of future offshore oil drilling, which fuels the adverse impacts of climate change. Unique coastal ecosystems from the Gulf of Mexico to Alaska’s Cook Inlet are at risk of being destroyed. These areas support thriving coastal economies, vibrant marine habitats, and a variety of ocean recreation activities, from kayaking, fishing, swimming, and surfing to beach days with your family.

It is crucial for us all to raise our voices in opposition to expanding offshore drilling. Link in bio to take action. #DrillingIsKilling

It’s been 15 years since the Deepwater Horizon oil spill in the Gulf of Mexico—a reminder of how the recklessness of the fossil fuel industry can lead to devastating, long-lasting impacts on marine life and coastal communities. Despite the risks, Big Oil wants to open our oceans to more offshore drilling and leave it to us to clean up their mess.

15 years after the BP Deepwater Horizon oil spill, coastal communities and ecosystems are still recovering—and the oil industry is still pushing to expand offshore drilling. Eleven people lost their lives from BP's disaster, which also killed an estimated 25,900 marine mammals, 82,000 birds, and 6,165 sea turtles. It caused $17.2 billion in damage and wiped out 25,000 jobs. Hazmat teams worked to disperse the oil, but the damage lingered for years.

Now, President Trump is illegally pushing to open nearly every U.S. coastline to oil drilling—risking another catastrophe. Earthjustice is suing the Trump administration to stop this unlawful move. ​

Today, we filed the first environmental lawsuit against the new Trump administration. There will be more to come. 

Amid a barrage of anti-environment actions on his first day in office, President Trump issued an order to revoke former president Biden’s protection of millions of acres of ocean from future oil and gas drilling. We’re challenging President Trump’s illegal attempt to sell our public waters to the oil and gas industry.

🔥Our hearts are breaking 💔 for all the loss resulting from the devastating Los Angeles Fires. Our co founder Dominique Browning posts about what this means.

#lafires #wildfires #momsactnow

For those reading conflicting statements about the fires raging in the Los Angeles area - the current environmental crisis that has risen to the top of the news - the cause of the fires and the response to them can be confusing to say the least. What is clear is that all Southern California is currently in moderate to severe drought (meaning plenty of dry brush and vegetation to act as fuel) and that powerful Santa Ana winds that blow from inland to the coast are fanning the flames. It's also clear that people are the cause of most fires in the area - and people have certainly moved into the more natural, hilly parts of Los Angeles County in recent years where these fires are burning. Condolences to those who have lost loved ones and homes.

That said, there is another narrative out there that's putting the blame in a different direction. Those claims - e.g., that the Los Angeles area fires are being caused by water distribution policies in northern California designed to protect smelt and there’s not enough water to fight fires or there are no FEMA funds available - are being examined by numerous fact-checkers and found to be questionable at best. As one wrote, "Regardless, this wildfire was caused by a combination of a drought, extremely high winds and long-ago decisions by planners and developers about how far into the hills to build..."

These environmental crises – from Southern California to South Florida - are going to be with us for a long time to come. We've always had them, but we live in a different, hotter world now (sometimes wetter, sometimes drier, almost always warmer than it was in the past). It's going to take knowledge and information to tackle these problems as best as we can.

Summary of the political firestorm taking place around the LA fires below including where the political narrative is contradicted by facts. Also - these posts (such as our recent post on offshore drilling) are not meant to promote or disparage politicians or parties. They are intended to encourage discussion and debate on environmental science and policy - what this environmental organization does.  There are real questions that should be answered about things like a longer and more intense fire season and more powerful Atlantic hurricanes.  Please try to keep comments to the policy aspects of this story (actual causes and recommended responses) as opposed to partisan politics. There is no election coming up so no point whatsoever in campaign-like statements. The question as to who would govern was decided back in early November. But there are certainly environmental problems out there that are going to be with us for a long time that will need to be resolved.

𝐇𝐮𝐦𝐚𝐧𝐢𝐭𝐚𝐫𝐢𝐚𝐧 𝐀𝐢𝐝 𝐚𝐧𝐝 𝐃𝐞𝐯𝐞𝐥𝐨𝐩𝐦𝐞𝐧𝐭: 𝐅𝐨𝐮𝐧𝐝𝐚𝐭𝐢𝐨𝐧𝐬 𝐚𝐧𝐝 𝐊𝐞𝐲 𝐂𝐨𝐧𝐜𝐞𝐩𝐭𝐬

𝗛𝘂𝗺𝗮𝗻𝗶𝘁𝗮𝗿𝗶𝗮𝗻 𝗮𝗶𝗱 refers to the provision of immediate, short-term assistance to people and regions in crisis, often due to natural disasters, armed conflict, or food insecurity. 𝗗𝗲𝘃𝗲𝗹𝗼𝗽𝗺𝗲𝗻𝘁 𝗮𝗶𝗱, on the other hand, refers to long-term support aimed at fostering economic growth, improving governance, and building infrastructure in developing countries. Over time, both forms of aid have become key aspects of U.S. foreign policy, reflecting broader ideological, geopolitical, and economic objectives.

𝙃𝙪𝙢𝙖𝙣𝙞𝙩𝙖𝙧𝙞𝙖𝙣 𝘼𝙞𝙙
• 𝘋𝘦𝘧𝘪𝘯𝘪𝘵𝘪𝘰𝘯: Immediate, short-term assistance provided to people in crisis situations, such as after natural disasters, armed conflicts, or pandemics.
• 𝘗𝘶𝘳𝘱𝘰𝘴𝘦: To save lives, alleviate suffering, and maintain human dignity during emergencies.
• 𝘌𝘹𝘢𝘮𝘱𝘭𝘦𝘴: Emergency food supplies, medical care, shelter after disasters, and aid to conflict-affected populations.
𝘿𝙚𝙫𝙚𝙡𝙤𝙥𝙢𝙚𝙣𝙩 𝘼𝙞𝙙
• 𝘋𝘦𝘧𝘪𝘯𝘪𝘵𝘪𝘰𝘯: Long-term support aimed at promoting sustainable economic, social, and political development in less developed countries.
• 𝘗𝘶𝘳𝘱𝘰𝘴𝘦: To address the root causes of poverty and inequality, improve governance, and build stable, self-sufficient communities.
• 𝘌𝘹𝘢𝘮𝘱𝘭𝘦𝘴: Building infrastructure (e.g., roads, schools, hospitals), education programs, economic reforms, and agricultural development.
                               ꘏ ꘏ ꘏ ꘏ ꘏ ꘏ ꘏

𝗘𝘃𝗼𝗹𝘂𝘁𝗶𝗼𝗻 𝗼𝗳 𝗔𝗶𝗱: 𝗙𝗿𝗼𝗺 𝗖𝗵𝗮𝗿𝗶𝘁𝘆 𝘁𝗼 𝗣𝗼𝗹𝗶𝗰𝘆
Initially, U.S. humanitarian efforts were largely driven by religious and charitable organizations, with a focus on missionary work and providing assistance to the poor both domestically and abroad. However, by the 20th century, aid became increasingly institutionalized, with the U.S. government playing a larger role in coordinating and funding relief and development initiatives. This shift reflected broader changes in U.S. foreign policy, where aid was no longer just an act of charity but also a strategic tool for promoting political stability, economic development, and, particularly during the Cold War, containing communism.

𝗣𝗿𝗲-𝟮𝟬𝘁𝗵 𝗖𝗲𝗻𝘁𝘂𝗿𝘆: 𝗧𝗵𝗲 𝗙𝗼𝘂𝗻𝗱𝗮𝘁𝗶𝗼𝗻𝘀 𝗼𝗳 𝗔𝗺𝗲𝗿𝗶𝗰𝗮𝗻 𝗛𝘂𝗺𝗮𝗻𝗶𝘁𝗮𝗿𝗶𝗮𝗻𝗶𝘀𝗺
Early Religious and Philanthropic Movements
The roots of American humanitarianism can be traced to the religious and philanthropic movements of the 18th and 19th centuries. Inspired by teaching of ethics and the 𝗦𝗲𝗰𝗼𝗻𝗱 𝗚𝗿𝗲𝗮𝘁 𝗔𝘄𝗮𝗸𝗲𝗻𝗶𝗻𝗴, religious groups began to see charity as an essential component of their faith. This period saw the rise of missionary work in both domestic and international contexts.

•𝙈𝙞𝙨𝙨𝙞𝙤𝙣𝙖𝙧𝙮 𝙎𝙤𝙘𝙞𝙚𝙩𝙞𝙚𝙨: Organizations like the American Board of Commissioners for Foreign Missions (ABCFM) were founded in the early 19th century to spread Christianity and provide relief to "heathen" populations in Africa, Asia, and the Middle East. These early efforts were often paternalistic and intertwined with the broader agenda of Western imperialism.
•𝘼𝙗𝙤𝙡𝙞𝙩𝙞𝙤𝙣𝙞𝙨𝙩 𝙈𝙤𝙫𝙚𝙢𝙚𝙣𝙩𝙨: Humanitarian impulses were also reflected in the abolitionist movement, which sought to end slavery in the United States. Figures like William Lloyd Garrison and Frederick Douglass were instrumental in framing the moral arguments against slavery, which were also tied to broader human rights concerns.

𝗨.𝗦. 𝗘𝘅𝗽𝗮𝗻𝘀𝗶𝗼𝗻 𝗮𝗻𝗱 𝘁𝗵𝗲 𝗠𝗶𝘀𝘀𝗶𝗼𝗻𝗮𝗿𝘆 𝗘𝘁𝗵𝗼𝘀
During the 19th century, the notion of 𝗠𝗮𝗻𝗶𝗳𝗲𝘀𝘁 𝗗𝗲𝘀𝘁𝗶𝗻𝘆-𝑡𝘩𝑒 𝑏𝑒𝑙𝑖𝑒𝑓 𝑡𝘩𝑎𝑡 𝑡𝘩𝑒 𝑈.𝑆. 𝑤𝑎𝑠 𝑑𝑒𝑠𝑡𝑖𝑛𝑒𝑑 𝑡𝑜 𝑒𝑥𝑝𝑎𝑛𝑑 𝑎𝑐𝑟𝑜𝑠𝑠 𝑡𝘩𝑒 𝑁𝑜𝑟𝑡𝘩 𝐴𝑚𝑒𝑟𝑖𝑐𝑎𝑛 𝑐𝑜𝑛𝑡𝑖𝑛𝑒𝑛𝑡-had a humanitarian dimension. Many Americans believed they had a moral duty to "civilize" indigenous populations and spread Christianity and democracy.

• 𝙉𝙖𝙩𝙞𝙫𝙚 𝘼𝙢𝙚𝙧𝙞𝙘𝙖𝙣 𝙍𝙚𝙡𝙤𝙘𝙖𝙩𝙞𝙤𝙣 Although framed as an act of benevolence, the forced relocation of Native Americans under policies like the 𝗜𝗻𝗱𝗶𝗮𝗻 𝗥𝗲𝗺𝗼𝘃𝗮𝗹 𝗔𝗰𝘁 𝗼𝗳 𝟭𝟴𝟯𝟬 was a form of coercive humanitarianism, where aid was tied to the broader goal of U.S. territorial expansion.
•𝘾𝙖𝙨𝙚 𝙎𝙩𝙪𝙙𝙮: 𝙏𝙝𝙚 𝘼𝙢𝙚𝙧𝙞𝙘𝙖𝙣 𝘾𝙤𝙡𝙤𝙣𝙞𝙯𝙖𝙩𝙞𝙤𝙣 𝙎𝙤𝙘𝙞𝙚𝙩𝙮 (𝟭𝟴𝟭𝟲)
One of the earliest examples of U.S. humanitarianism with global implications was the 𝗔𝗺𝗲𝗿𝗶𝗰𝗮𝗻 𝗖𝗼𝗹𝗼𝗻𝗶𝘇𝗮𝘁𝗶𝗼𝗻 𝗦𝗼𝗰𝗶𝗲𝘁𝘆 (ACS), founded in 1816. The ACS aimed to resettle freed African Americans in Africa, specifically in what would become Liberia. While presented as a humanitarian gesture, the ACS was also motivated by racist beliefs that freed slaves could not coexist with white Americans. The colonization of Liberia, which was framed as a "𝑐𝑖𝑣𝑖𝑙𝑖𝑧𝑖𝑛𝑔 𝑚𝑖𝑠𝑠𝑖𝑜𝑛," laid the groundwork for later U.S. interventions in Africa, where aid and diplomacy were often intertwined with economic and political interests. 
                            
𝗪𝗼𝗿𝗹𝗱 𝗪𝗮𝗿 𝗜 𝗮𝗻𝗱 𝘁𝗵𝗲 𝗕𝗶𝗿𝘁𝗵 𝗼𝗳 𝗠𝗼𝗱𝗲𝗿𝗻 𝗛𝘂𝗺𝗮𝗻𝗶𝘁𝗮𝗿𝗶𝗮𝗻𝗶𝘀𝗺

𝗧𝗵𝗲 𝗔𝗺𝗲𝗿𝗶𝗰𝗮𝗻 𝗥𝗲𝗱 𝗖𝗿𝗼𝘀𝘀 𝗮𝗻𝗱 𝗨.𝗦. 𝗜𝗻𝘃𝗼𝗹𝘃𝗲𝗺𝗲𝗻𝘁 𝗶𝗻 𝗘𝘂𝗿𝗼𝗽𝗲𝗮𝗻 𝗥𝗲𝗹𝗶𝗲𝗳 𝗘𝗳𝗳𝗼𝗿𝘁𝘀
World War I marked the first major instance of the
U.S. government and private organizations coordinating large-scale international humanitarian efforts. The American Red Cross, founded in 1881 by Clara Barton, played a critical role in providing medical and relief services to European countries devastated by the war.

• 𝙏𝙝𝙚 𝙍𝙚𝙙 𝘾𝙧𝙤𝙨𝙨'𝙨 𝙍𝙤𝙡𝙚: During World War I, the American Red Cross significantly expanded its operations, providing relief to soldiers and civilians alike. By the end of the war, it had over 20 million members and had raised more than $400 million for war relief efforts. 

𝗛𝗲𝗿𝗯𝗲𝗿𝘁 𝗛𝗼𝗼𝘃𝗲𝗿 𝗮𝗻𝗱 𝘁𝗵𝗲 𝗔𝗺𝗲𝗿𝗶𝗰𝗮𝗻 𝗥𝗲𝗹𝗶𝗲𝗳 𝗔𝗱𝗺𝗶𝗻𝗶𝘀𝘁𝗿𝗮𝘁𝗶𝗼𝗻 (𝗔𝗥𝗔)
Herbert Hoover, a mining engineer and businessman, played a pivotal role in the U.S. government's humanitarian efforts during and after World War I. As head of the American Relief Administration (ARA), Hoover oversaw the delivery of food and medical aid to millions of people in war-torn Europe.

• 𝘽𝙚𝙡𝙜𝙞𝙖𝙣 𝙍𝙚𝙡𝙞𝙚𝙛 𝙀𝙛𝙛𝙤𝙧𝙩 (1914-1918): Hoover first gained prominence during World War I by organizing the 𝗖𝗼𝗺𝗺𝗶𝘀𝘀𝗶𝗼𝗻 𝗳𝗼𝗿 𝗥𝗲𝗹𝗶𝗲𝗳 𝗶𝗻 𝗕𝗲𝗹𝗴𝗶𝘂𝗺 (CRB), which provided food and supplies to Belgian civilians trapped behind German lines. The CRB is often cited as one of the first large-scale international relief efforts and set the stage for future U.S. humanitarian interventions.

𝘾𝙖𝙨𝙚 𝙎𝙩𝙪𝙙𝙮: 𝙏𝙝𝙚 𝘽𝙚𝙡𝙜𝙞𝙪𝙢 𝙍𝙚𝙡𝙞𝙚𝙛 𝙀𝙛𝙛𝙤𝙧𝙩 (1914-1918)
During World War I, the small, neutral country of Belgium became a battleground as German forces invaded. The Belgian people were cut off from food supplies, and mass starvation was imminent.
Hoover's Commission for Relief in Belgium became the largest humanitarian aid operation of its time, providing billions of pounds of food to more than 9 million people. This set a precedent for the U.S. role in international humanitarian relief, showing that aid could be mobilized on a global scale.
𝗧𝗵𝗲 𝗚𝗿𝗲𝗮𝘁 𝗗𝗲𝗽𝗿𝗲𝘀𝘀𝗶𝗼𝗻 𝗮𝗻𝗱 𝘁𝗵𝗲 𝗡𝗲𝘄 𝗗𝗲𝗮𝗹: 𝗗𝗼𝗺𝗲𝘀𝘁𝗶𝗰 𝗗𝗲𝘃𝗲𝗹𝗼𝗽𝗺𝗲𝗻𝘁 𝗠𝗼𝗱𝗲𝗹𝘀 

𝗧𝗵𝗲 𝗧𝗲𝗻𝗻𝗲𝘀𝘀𝗲𝗲 𝗩𝗮𝗹𝗹𝗲𝘆 𝗔𝘂𝘁𝗵𝗼𝗿𝗶𝘁𝘆 (𝗧𝗩𝗔) 𝗮𝗻𝗱 𝗥𝘂𝗿𝗮𝗹 𝗗𝗲𝘃𝗲𝗹𝗼𝗽𝗺𝗲𝗻𝘁
The U.S. response to the 𝗚𝗿𝗲𝗮𝘁 𝗗𝗲𝗽𝗿𝗲𝘀𝘀𝗶𝗼𝗻 in the 1930s provided important lessons for later international development efforts. The Tennessee Valley Authority (TVA), created in 1933, was one of the most ambitious projects of the 𝗡𝗲𝘄 𝗗𝗲𝗮𝗹. It aimed to modernize the Tennessee Valley, one of the poorest regions in the United States, by building dams, providing electricity, and promoting agricultural development.

• 𝙍𝙪𝙧𝙖𝙡 𝙀𝙡𝙚𝙘𝙩𝙧𝙞𝙛𝙞𝙘𝙖𝙩𝙞𝙤𝙣: The TVA brought electricity to rural areas, which in turn stimulated local economies. This model of infrastructure development influenced later U.S. efforts to promote economic growth in developing countries.

𝗧𝗵𝗲 𝗖𝗶𝘃𝗶𝗹𝗶𝗮𝗻 𝗖𝗼𝗻𝘀𝗲𝗿𝘃𝗮𝘁𝗶𝗼𝗻 𝗖𝗼𝗿𝗽𝘀 (𝗖𝗖𝗖)
Another key New Deal program was the Civilian Conservation Corps (CCC), which employed young men in public works projects, such as planting trees, building parks, and combating soil erosion. The CCC not only provided jobs during the Depression but also contributed to long-term environmental conservation, a theme that would later be incorporated into international development programs.

𝗟𝗲𝘀𝘀𝗼𝗻𝘀 𝗳𝗼𝗿 𝗜𝗻𝘁𝗲𝗿𝗻𝗮𝘁𝗶𝗼𝗻𝗮𝗹 𝗗𝗲𝘃𝗲𝗹𝗼𝗽𝗺𝗲𝗻𝘁
The New Deal illustrated the potential of 𝘀𝘁𝗮𝘁𝗲-𝗹𝗲𝗱 𝗱𝗲𝘃𝗲𝗹𝗼𝗽𝗺𝗲𝗻𝘁 to alleviate poverty and promote economic growth. Later, U.S. foreign aid programs, such as the Marshall Plan and USAID, would adopt many of these principles, particularly the emphasis on infrastructure development and government intervention in stimulating economic activity.

𝗪𝗼𝗿𝗹𝗱 𝗪𝗮𝗿 𝗜𝗹 𝗮𝗻𝗱 𝘁𝗵𝗲 𝗣𝗼𝘀𝘁𝘄𝗮𝗿 𝗘𝗿𝗮: 𝗧𝗵𝗲 𝗘𝗺𝗲𝗿𝗴𝗲𝗻𝗰𝗲 𝗼𝗳 𝗨.𝗦. 𝗙𝗼𝗿𝗲𝗶𝗴𝗻 𝗔𝗶𝗱

𝗧𝗵𝗲 𝗠𝗮𝗿𝘀𝗵𝗮𝗹𝗹 𝗣𝗹𝗮𝗻 and the Reconstruction of Europe
The Marshall Plan (1948-1952) was a landmark U.S. foreign aid initiative that provided more than $13 billion (equivalent to over $130 billion today) to help rebuild Western Europe after World War II. Officially known as the European Recovery Program, the Marshall Plan was designed to revive war-torn economies, prevent the spread of communism, and stabilize the region.

• 𝙀𝙘𝙤𝙣𝙤𝙢𝙞𝙘 𝙂𝙧𝙤𝙬𝙩𝙝: The Marshall Plan was successful in restoring economic growth in countries like Germany, France, and Italy, significantly reducing the appeal of communism in these nations.

• 𝘼𝙞𝙙 𝙖𝙨 𝙂𝙚𝙤𝙥𝙤𝙡𝙞𝙩𝙞𝙘𝙨: Although humanitarian in nature, the Marshall Plan was also a strategic tool to counter Soviet influence in Europe durina the earl stades of the Cold War.

𝗧𝗵𝗲 𝗕𝗿𝗲𝘁𝘁𝗼𝗻 𝗪𝗼𝗼𝗱𝘀 𝗜𝗻𝘀𝘁𝗶𝘁𝘂𝘁𝗶𝗼𝗻𝘀: 𝗪𝗼𝗿𝗹𝗱 𝗕𝗮𝗻𝗸, 𝗜𝗠𝗙, 𝗮𝗻𝗱 𝗚𝗔𝗧𝗧
The postwar period also saw the creation of the Bretton Woods Institutions-the 𝗪𝗼𝗿𝗹𝗱 𝗕𝗮𝗻𝗸, the 𝗜𝗻𝘁𝗲𝗿𝗻𝗮𝘁𝗶𝗼𝗻𝗮𝗹 𝗠𝗼𝗻𝗲𝘁𝗮𝗿𝘆 𝗙𝘂𝗻𝗱 (IMF), and the 𝗚𝗲𝗻𝗲𝗿𝗮𝗹 𝗔𝗴𝗿𝗲𝗲𝗺𝗲𝗻𝘁 𝗼𝗻 𝗧𝗮𝗿𝗶𝗳𝗳𝘀 𝗮𝗻𝗱 𝗧𝗿𝗮𝗱𝗲 (GATT)-which were designed to stabilize the global economy and promote international development.

𝗪𝗼𝗿𝗹𝗱 𝗕𝗮𝗻𝗸: Initially focused on rebuilding
Europe, the World Bank later shifted its focus to providing loans and technical assistance to developing countries.

𝗜𝗠𝗙: The IMF was created to provide short-term financial assistance to countries facing balance-of-payments crises and to promote international monetary cooperation.

𝗚𝗔𝗧𝗧/𝗪𝗧𝗢: The GATT, which later became the World Trade Organization (WTO), was established to promote free trade and reduce tariffs, which were seen as essential for global economic development. The Truman Doctrine and the Start of the Cold War
The Truman Doctrine, announced in 1947, articulated a new U.S. foreign policy aimed at containing the spread of communism. Under this doctrine, the U.S. pledged to provide military and economic aid to countries threatened by communist insurgencies, particularly Greece and Turkey. This marked the beginning of U.S. foreign aid as a tool of Cold War strategy.

𝗖𝗮𝘀𝗲 𝗦𝘁𝘂𝗱𝘆: 𝗨.𝗦. 𝗔𝗶𝗱 𝗶𝗻 𝗚𝗿𝗲𝗲𝗰𝗲 𝗮𝗻𝗱 𝗧𝘂𝗿𝗸𝗲𝘆 (𝟭𝟵𝟰𝟳)
In 1947, the U.S. provided $400 million in aid to Greece and Turkey to help them resist communist insurgencies. This aid included military assistance, economic development programs, and humanitarian relief. The success of this intervention in stabilizing these countries helped solidify the use of foreign aid as a key component of U.S. Cold War strategy.

𝗧𝗵𝗲 𝗖𝗼𝗹𝗱 𝗪𝗮𝗿 𝗘𝗿𝗮: 𝗔𝗶𝗱 𝗮𝘀 𝗮 𝗦𝘁𝗿𝗮𝘁𝗲𝗴𝗶𝗰 𝗜𝗻𝘀𝘁𝗿𝘂𝗺𝗲𝗻𝘁

𝗧𝗵𝗲 𝗙𝗼𝘂𝗻𝗱𝗶𝗻𝗴 𝗼𝗳 𝗨𝗦𝗔𝗜𝗗 (𝟭𝟵𝟲𝟭) 𝗮𝗻𝗱 𝘁𝗵𝗲 𝗔𝗹𝗹𝗶𝗮𝗻𝗰𝗲 𝗳𝗼𝗿 𝗣𝗿𝗼𝗴𝗿𝗲𝘀𝘀 
The creation of the 𝗨.𝗦. 𝗔𝗴𝗲𝗻𝗰𝘆 𝗳𝗼𝗿 𝗜𝗻𝘁𝗲𝗿𝗻𝗮𝘁𝗶𝗼𝗻𝗮𝗹 𝗗𝗲𝘃𝗲𝗹𝗼𝗽𝗺𝗲𝗻𝘁 (USAID) in 1961 marked the institutionalization of U.S. foreign aid. President 𝗝𝗼𝗵𝗻 𝗙. 𝗞𝗲𝗻𝗻𝗲𝗱𝘆 established USAID to coordinate and administer U.S. development assistance. Around the same time, Kennedy launched the Alliance for Progress, a ten-year plan to promote economic development and social reforms in Latin America.

• 𝘼𝙡𝙡𝙞𝙖𝙣𝙘𝙚 𝙛𝙤𝙧 𝙋𝙧𝙤𝙜𝙧𝙚𝙨𝙨: The Alliance for Progress aimed to reduce poverty and inequality in Latin America, thereby reducing the appeal of communism. However, the program had mixed results, with some countries experiencing significant growth while others achieved little progress. Food for Peace (PL 480) and the Green Revolution The Food for Peace Program (Public Law 480), launched in 1954, aimed to use U.S. agricultural surpluses to provide food aid to developing countries. This program laid the groundwork for the Green Revolution, a series of agricultural innovations that dramatically increased food production in Latin America and Asia.

• 𝙂𝙧𝙚𝙚𝙣 𝙍𝙚𝙫𝙤𝙡𝙪𝙩𝙞𝙤𝙣: The Green Revolution, which began in the 1960s, involved the introduction of high-yield crop varieties, modern irrigation techniques, and chemical fertilizers. While it succeeded in increasing food production, it also had significant environmental and social consequences, such as increased inequality among farmers.

𝗧𝗵𝗲 𝗥𝗼𝗹𝗲 𝗼𝗳 𝗡𝗼𝗻-𝗚𝗼𝘃𝗲𝗿𝗻𝗺𝗲𝗻𝘁𝗮𝗹 𝗢𝗿𝗴𝗮𝗻𝗶𝘇𝗮𝘁𝗶𝗼𝗻𝘀 (𝗡𝗚𝗢𝘀) 𝗗𝘂𝗿𝗶𝗻𝗴 𝘁𝗵𝗲 𝗖𝗼𝗹𝗱 𝗪𝗮𝗿
During the Cold War, 𝗡𝗼𝗻-𝗚𝗼𝘃𝗲𝗿𝗻𝗺𝗲𝗻𝘁𝗮𝗹 𝗢𝗿𝗴𝗮𝗻𝗶𝘇𝗮𝘁𝗶𝗼𝗻𝘀 (NGOs) played an increasingly important role in U.S. humanitarian and development aid. Organizations like 𝗖𝗔𝗥𝗘, 𝗢𝘅𝗳𝗮𝗺, and 𝗖𝗮𝘁𝗵𝗼𝗹𝗶𝗰 𝗥𝗲𝗹𝗶𝗲𝗳 𝗦𝗲𝗿𝘃𝗶𝗰𝗲𝘀 partnered with the U.S. government to deliver aid in conflict zones and developing countries.

• 𝘾𝘼𝙍𝙀 𝙋𝙖𝙘𝙠𝙖𝙜𝙚𝙨: One of the most iconic examples of NGO-led humanitarian aid was the
CARE Package, which provided food and supplies to people in postwar Europe and later in developing countries. CARE became a key partner of USAID, helping to deliver food aid, medical supplies, and educational materials.

𝘾𝙖𝙨𝙚 𝙎𝙩𝙪𝙙𝙮: U.S. Aid in Vietnam and Southeast Asia
The U.S. intervention in Vietnam (1955-1975) highlighted the complexities of using aid as a tool of military strategy. In addition to military aid, the U.S. provided billions of dollars in economic development assistance to South Vietnam in an attempt to create a stable, anti-communist state.

• 𝙎𝙩𝙧𝙖𝙩𝙚𝙜𝙞𝙘 𝙃𝙖𝙢𝙡𝙚𝙩 𝙋𝙧𝙤𝙜𝙧𝙖𝙢: One of the most controversial aid programs was the Strategic Hamlet Program, which aimed to relocate rural populations into fortified villages to prevent them from being influenced by the Viet Cong. However, the program was largely unsuccessful and contributed to growing resentment against the U.S. and the South Vietnamese government. 

•𝙋𝙤𝙨𝙩-𝙒𝙖𝙧 𝙍𝙚𝙘𝙤𝙣𝙨𝙩𝙧𝙪𝙘𝙩𝙞𝙤𝙣 After the war, the U.S. also provided aid to neighboring countries like Cambodia and Laos, which had been heavily impacted by the conflict. This aid included both humanitarian relief for refugees and long-term development assistance.
                               ꘏ ꘏ ꘏ ꘏ ꘏ ꘏ ꘏

𝗣𝗼𝘀𝘁-𝗖𝗼𝗹𝗱 𝗪𝗮𝗿: 𝗧𝗵𝗲 𝗦𝗵𝗶𝗳𝘁 𝗳𝗿𝗼𝗺 𝗖𝗼𝗻𝘁𝗮𝗶𝗻𝗺𝗲𝗻𝘁 𝘁𝗼 𝗗𝗲𝘃𝗲𝗹𝗼𝗽𝗺𝗲𝗻𝘁

𝗧𝗵𝗲 𝗪𝗮𝘀𝗵𝗶𝗻𝗴𝘁𝗼𝗻 𝗖𝗼𝗻𝘀𝗲𝗻𝘀𝘂𝘀 𝗮𝗻𝗱 𝗦𝘁𝗿𝘂𝗰𝘁𝘂𝗿𝗮𝗹 𝗔𝗱𝗷𝘂𝘀𝘁𝗺𝗲𝗻𝘁 𝗣𝗿𝗼𝗴𝗿𝗮𝗺𝘀 (𝗦𝗔𝗣𝘀) 
In the late 1980s and 1990s, U.S. development policy was heavily influenced by the 𝗪𝗮𝘀𝗵𝗶𝗻𝗴𝘁𝗼𝗻 𝗖𝗼𝗻𝘀𝗲𝗻𝘀𝘂𝘀, a set of economic reforms that promoted 𝗳𝗿𝗲𝗲 𝗺𝗮𝗿𝗸𝗲𝘁𝘀, 𝗽𝗿𝗶𝘃𝗮𝘁𝗶𝘇𝗮𝘁𝗶𝗼𝗻, and 𝗱𝗲𝗿𝗲𝗴𝘂𝗹𝗮𝘁𝗶𝗼𝗻. These ideas were enforced through 𝗦𝘁𝗿𝘂𝗰𝘁𝘂𝗿𝗮𝗹 𝗔𝗱𝗷𝘂𝘀𝘁𝗺𝗲𝗻𝘁 𝗣𝗿𝗼𝗴𝗿𝗮𝗺𝘀 (SAPs), often administered by the World Bank and IMF.

• 𝗦𝗔𝗣𝘀: Structural Adjustment Programs required developing countries to implement austerity measures, such as cutting government spending and privatizing state-owned enterprises, in exchange for financial assistance. While these programs were intended to stabilize economies, they were widely criticized for exacerbating poverty and inequality.

𝗛𝘂𝗺𝗮𝗻𝗶𝘁𝗮𝗿𝗶𝗮𝗻 𝗜𝗻𝘁𝗲𝗿𝘃𝗲𝗻𝘁𝗶𝗼𝗻𝘀 𝗶𝗻 𝘁𝗵𝗲 𝟭𝟵𝟵𝟬𝘀: 𝗦𝗼𝗺𝗮𝗹𝗶𝗮, 𝗛𝗮𝗶𝘁𝗶, 𝗮𝗻𝗱 𝘁𝗵𝗲 𝗕𝗮𝗹𝗸𝗮𝗻𝘀 
The end of the Cold War brought a new focus on humanitarian interventions in conflict zones. U.S. military and aid interventions in Somalia (1992), Haiti (1994), and the Balkans (1995) were framed as efforts to protect human rights and promote peace, though they often had mixed results.

𝙎𝙤𝙢𝙖𝙡𝙞𝙖 (1992-1993): The U.S. intervention in Somalia, known as Operation Restore Hope, aimed to provide humanitarian relief to civilians suffering from famine and civil war. However, the mission quickly became entangled in local conflicts, culminating in the Battle of Mogadishu in 1993, which led to the withdrawal of U.S. forces.

𝙃𝙖𝙞𝙩𝙞 (1994): In 1994, U.S. forces intervened in Haiti to restore the democratically-elected government of Jean-Bertrand Aristide, who had been overthrown in a military coup. While the intervention successfully reinstated Aristide, Haiti continued to face significant political and economic challenges.

𝘽𝙖𝙡𝙠𝙖𝙣𝙨 (1995): U.S. involvement in the Balkans, particularly in Bosnia and Kosovo, was motivated by the desire to prevent ethnic cleansing and promote regional stability. The U.S. played a key role in brokering the Dayton Accords, which ended the Bosnian War in 1995.

𝗧𝗵𝗲 𝗥𝗶𝘀𝗲 𝗼𝗳 𝗚𝗹𝗼𝗯𝗮𝗹 𝗛𝗲𝗮𝗹𝘁𝗵: 𝗛𝗜𝗩/𝗔𝗜𝗗𝗦 𝗮𝗻𝗱 𝗣𝗘𝗣𝗙𝗔𝗥
In the late 1990s and early 2000s, global health became a major focus of U.S. foreign aid. The President's Emergency Plan for AIDS Relief (PEPFAR), launched by President George W. Bush in 2003, committed billions of dollars to combat the HIV/AIDS epidemic, particularly in Sub-Saharan Africa.

• 𝙋𝙀𝙋𝙁𝘼𝙍: PEPFAR is widely regarded as one of the most successful U.S. foreign aid programs, helping to provide antiretroviral treatment to millions of people living with HIV/AIDS. The program also made significant investments in health infrastructure and prevention efforts, such as distributing condoms and promoting safe sex practices.

𝘾𝙖𝙨𝙚 𝙎𝙩𝙪𝙙𝙮: U.S. Intervention in Somalia (1992-1993)
The U.S. intervention in Somalia, also known as Operation Restore Hope, was one of the most significant humanitarian missions of the post-Cold era. Initially aimed at providing food aid to civilians affected by famine and civil war, the mission quickly became entangled in local power struggles.

• 𝘽𝙖𝙩𝙩𝙡𝙚 𝙤𝙛 𝙈𝙤𝙜𝙖𝙙𝙞𝙨𝙝𝙪: The mission culminated in the Battle of Mogadishu in October 1993, when U.S. forces clashed with Somali militias loyal to warlord 𝗠𝗼𝗵𝗮𝗺𝗲𝗱 𝗙𝗮𝗿𝗿𝗮𝗵 𝗔𝗶𝗱𝗶𝗱. The downing of two U.S. Black Hawk helicopters and the deaths of 18 American soldiers during this battle became the focal point of U.S. involvement in Somalia. The incident, popularly known as "Black Hawk Down," led to a public outcry in the U.S., and ultimately, the withdrawal of American troops from Somalia.

• 𝘾𝙤𝙣𝙨𝙚𝙦𝙪𝙚𝙣𝙘𝙚𝙨: The failure of the mission in Somalia had lasting impacts on U.S. foreign policy. It led to a more cautious approach toward humanitarian interventions, often referred to as the "Somalia Syndrome." This reluctance to intervene in complex humanitarian crises is believed to have influenced U.S. inaction during the Rwandan Genocide in 1994, where an estimated 800,000 people were killed.
